startScreenOptions.title: AN34.1-AN34.3 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 2 embedded questions for AN34.1-AN34.3.

interactions.label: Why is the lingual nerve particularly vulnerable during surgical removal of a su

params.question: Why is the lingual nerve particularly vulnerable during surgical removal of a submandibular stone from Wharton's duct?

answers.text: It runs superficial to the mylohyoid and is easily exposed during incision

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The lingual nerve lies deep to the mylohyoid in the sublingual space, not superficial to it. Its vulnerability comes from its intimate relationship with Wharton's duct, not its depth.

answers.text: It crosses Wharton's duct twice — first lateral, then inferior — ending medial to the duct

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The lingual nerve first runs lateral to Wharton's duct, then crosses beneath it to end medial. This double crossing places the nerve directly in the surgical field during stone removal or gland excision.

answers.text: It provides the sole motor supply to the tongue, making any injury devastating

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Motor supply to the tongue is from the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII), not the lingual nerve. The lingual nerve carries general sensation and taste (via chorda tympani) to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue.

answers.text: It enters the gland substance directly to relay parasympathetic secretomotor fibres

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Parasympathetic relay occurs in the submandibular ganglion, which is suspended from the lingual nerve outside the gland. The nerve itself does not enter the gland substance.

overallFeedback.feedback: The lingual nerve crosses Wharton's duct twice — lateral to medial — placing it at direct risk during duct surgery. This is one of the most important relationships in submandibular region anatomy.
Well done. The lingual nerve's double crossing of Wharton's duct is a critical anatomical relationship that every surgeon must respect during submandibular surgery.

interactions.label: Which combination of factors best explains why 80–90% of salivary gland stones f

params.question: Which combination of factors best explains why 80–90% of salivary gland stones form in the submandibular gland?

answers.text: Short horizontal duct, serous saliva, and a wide orifice at the sublingual papilla

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is almost the opposite of reality. Wharton's duct is long (~5 cm) and courses uphill, submandibular saliva is mucous-rich (not purely serous), and the duct narrows rather than widens at the mylohyoid.

answers.text: Multiple small ducts, acidic secretions, and high serous content

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Multiple small ducts (ducts of Rivinus) describes the sublingual gland, not the submandibular. Submandibular saliva is alkaline, not acidic, and is mucous-rich — both factors that favour calcium salt precipitation.

answers.text: Long uphill duct, mucous-rich alkaline saliva, and duct narrowing at the mylohyoid

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Wharton's duct courses uphill against gravity (causing stasis), the saliva is mucous-rich and alkaline (favouring calcium phosphate precipitation), and the duct narrows at the mylohyoid (creating a bottleneck). These three factors converge to make the submandibular gland overwhelmingly stone-prone.

answers.text: Parasympathetic overstimulation from the chorda tympani causing excessive calcification

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Parasympathetic stimulation via the chorda tympani increases saliva flow, which would actually reduce stasis. Stone formation results from duct anatomy and saliva composition, not nerve overstimulation.

overallFeedback.feedback: Three factors converge: (1) Wharton's duct runs uphill against gravity, promoting stasis; (2) the saliva is mucous-rich and alkaline, favouring calcium precipitation; (3) the duct narrows at the mylohyoid. Together they allow calcium phosphate to crystallise around an organic nidus.
Excellent. Understanding these three converging factors — gravity-defying duct, mucous-alkaline saliva, and mylohyoid bottleneck — is the anatomical basis of submandibular sialolithiasis.
